coredata.dc:description: High-intensity light induces oxidative stress in plants, disrupting the photosynthetic system and causing photoinhibition. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) with wide absorption (300–700 nm) and low fluorescence quantum efficiency (2.11 %) effectively absorb excess sunlight around Chlorella pyrenoidosa. Additionally, the surface functional groups of CDs bind to free radicals, scavenging reactive oxygen species (ROS). Under high-intensity light stress, CDs activate signal transduction (hormones and MAPK), initiating diterpenoid pathways and antioxidant processes to scavenge ROS and enhance photosynthetic capacity through light absorption and heat dissipation mechanisms. Specifically, CDs up-regulate CpGA20ox expression, enhance the AsA-GSH cycle, and increase the SOD and CAT activities by 24.15 % and 136.0 %, respectively, reducing ROS levels by 52.0 %. Furthermore, CDs down-regulate the expression of light-harvesting proteins (CpLHCb2 and CpLcyB) while increasing the serine/threonine kinase-encoded genes to promote state transition from PSII to PSI, reducing exciton accumulation at QA. Moreover, CDs increase pigment content (chlorophyll a, b, total chlorophyll, and carotenoids by 2.24, 2.00, 1.42, and 2.17 times, respectively) and elevate the expression of carbon fixation genes (CpALDO and CpGAPDH). Ultimately, CDs enhance the antioxidant and photosynthetic capacities of Chlorella pyrenoidosa, increasing its biomass by 5.85 % (compared to that under normal light) and 17.1 % (compared to that under high-intensity light).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of CDs absorbing excess sunlight, alleviating damage from high-intensity light, and enhancing photosynthesis. Red fonts indicate an increase and green fonts indicate a decrease. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) HRTEM image of CDs. (B) Size distribution histogram of CDs. (C) Lattice fringe image of CDs. (D) Excitation and emission spectra of CDs. (E) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs in water. (F) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs in BG-11 medium over time.

(A) The photograph of the biomass, (B) growth curves, (C-F) chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, carotenoid, and total chlorophyll content, (G) oxygen-evolution of C. pyrenoidosa. Note: CK: natural light (4000 Lux). 0: high-intensity light stress (10000 Lux). 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, and 80 groups: high-intensity light stress with different concentrations of CDs. Data are the means of six replicates with SD, and different letters indicate a significant difference (P < 0.05).

(A-B) Light intensity and spectra of radiation covering the surface of C. pyrenoidosa and CDs/C. pyrenoidosa. (C) ABS/RC, DI0 /RC, TR0 /RC, ET0 /RC ET0 /TR0, and Fv/Fm of C. pyrenoidosa and CDs/C. pyrenoidosa. Each indicator's value is numerically depicted, with data normalized on a scale from 0 to 1 for each row. The color intensity varies according to the value size, clearly distinguishing between different treatments. (D-H) Y(II), ETR, qP, NPQ, and Y(NO) of C. pyrenoidosa and CDs/C. pyrenoidosa. (I) Scheme of the effect of CDs on light absorption and energy distribution in photosystems. The red arrows represent positive or negative effects caused by CDs. CK: natural light (4000 Lux). 0: high-intensity light stress (10000 Lux). 20, 40, and 80 groups: high-intensity light stress with different concentrations of CD. Data are the means of six replicates with SD, and different letters indicate a significant difference (P < 0.05). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A-B) ROS and O2 •− contents, (C) •OH degradation rates, (D)MDA contents, (E-H) SOD, CAT, APX, and POD activities, (I-K) AsA, AsA + DHA contents, and AsA/DHA ratios, (L) GSH of C. pyrenoidosa with different treatments. CK: natural light (4000 lux). 0: High-intensity light stress (10000 lux). 20, 40, and 80 groups: high-intensity light stress with different concentrations of CDs. Data are the means of six replicates with SD, and different letters indicate a significant difference (P < 0.05).

(A) The enrichment results of KEGG and the pathways associated with plant stress resistance and carbon fixation were presented in five dimensions. (B) Molecular mechanism of CDs alleviating light stress and promoting photosynthesis in C. pyrenoidosa. By Figdraw. Note: arcs indicate gene enrichment on the pathway, and heat maps indicate gene log2 FC values. “Cp” indicates C. pyrenoidosa, slanted fonts indicate genes, red fonts for up-regulation, and blue fonts for down-regulation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Carbon dots alleviate photoinhibition and enhance photosynthesis in Chlorella pyrenoidosa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-intensity light induces oxidative stress in plants, disrupting the photosynthetic system and causing photoinhibition. Herein, carbon dots (CDs) with wide absorption (300–700 nm) and low fluorescence quantum efficiency (2.11 %) effectively absorb excess sunlight around Chlorella pyrenoidosa. Additionally, the surface functional groups of CDs bind to free radicals, scavenging reactive oxygen species (ROS). Under high-intensity light stress, CDs activate signal transduction (hormones and MAPK), initiating diterpenoid pathways and antioxidant processes to scavenge ROS and enhance photosynthetic capacity through light absorption and heat dissipation mechanisms. Specifically, CDs up-regulate CpGA20ox expression, enhance the AsA-GSH cycle, and increase the SOD and CAT activities by 24.15 % and 136.0 %, respectively, reducing ROS levels by 52.0 %. Furthermore, CDs down-regulate the expression of light-harvesting proteins (CpLHCb2 and CpLcyB) while increasing the serine/threonine kinase-encoded genes to promote state transition from PSII to PSI, reducing exciton accumulation at QA. Moreover, CDs increase pigment content (chlorophyll a, b, total chlorophyll, and carotenoids by 2.24, 2.00, 1.42, and 2.17 times, respectively) and elevate the expression of carbon fixation genes (CpALDO and CpGAPDH). Ultimately, CDs enhance the antioxidant and photosynthetic capacities of Chlorella pyrenoidosa, increasing its biomass by 5.85 % (compared to that under normal light) and 17.1 % (compared to that under high-intensity light).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the worsening energy crisis, bioenergy has gained significant attention as a viable alternative to traditional energy sources. Chlorella pyrenoidosa (C. pyrenoidosa), a unicellular green microalga, is notable for its exceptional oil yield of up to 105 L·hm2·y-1, which is 20 times higher than that of soybeans [1]. Consequently, it has emerged as a promising candidate for biofuel research [2,3]. However, the growth of C. pyrenoidosa cells is affected by various environmental parameters, particularly light intensity [4,5]. Studies have shown that productivity losses due to fluctuations in light intensity can reach up to 40 % in different plant species [6–8]. Moreover, during summer, direct sunlight can produce light intensities ranging from 10,000 to 100,000 lux, while outdoor conditions without direct sunlight range from 1000 to 10,000 lux. Notably, C. pyrenoidosa’s biological functions and biomass significantly decline when exposed to light intensities exceeding 9,000 lux, indicating high-intensity light stress [9]. Therefore, to improve the production efficiency and energy effectiveness of microalgae, it is essential to minimize their exposure to excessive solar radiation and effectively mitigate the stress caused by high-intensity light.

High-intensity light stress causes photooxidative cell damage and photoinhibition in algae and plants [10,11]. This imbalance in energy distribution between photosystem I (PSI) and photosystem II (PSII) results in a rapid decline in photosynthetic efficiency, significantly limiting the growth and development of algae and plants [10,11]. ROS are considered to be major regulatory molecules in plants and function in early signaling processes. However, high-intensity light stress can lead to their overproduction, resulting in oxidative damage [12]. Plants possess their own ROS scavenging system to maintain a balance between ROS production and removal, ensuring normal growth and development. This system includes low-molecular-weight antioxidants such as ascorbic acid (AsA) and glutathione (GSH), as well as antioxidant enzymes like catalase (CAT), superoxide dismutase (SOD), peroxidase (POD), and glutathione reductase (GR) [13–15]. Additionally, ROS primarily accumulate within chloroplasts, and their massive accumulation also impairs adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and triphosphopyridine nucleotide (NADPH) production via the electron transport chain, damaging the photosynthetic apparatus, and reducing photosynthetic efficiency [16]. In the face of excessive light and potential damage to the photosynthetic mechanism, the self-protection mechanisms of plants are insufficient to combat extreme environmental stress. Currently, certain stimulators such as anthocyanin [17], melatonin [18], strigolactones [19] have been shown to enhance plant tolerance to high-intensity light by scavenging ROS and strengthening photosynthesis. However, there is little research aimed at reducing solar radiation to protect plants under high-intensity light conditions and the underlying mechanisms regarding the interaction with the defense system remain unclear.

Carbon dots (CDs), a type of nanomaterial, are distinguished by their small size, excellent biocompatibility, and abundant surface defects and unpaired electrons. These characteristics enable CDs to act as proton donors and scavengers of free radicals [20–22]. Studies have shown that CDs can mitigate ROS induced by abiotic stresses, such as salt and heat, by enhancing antioxidant systems [14,21,23,24]. Additionally, CDs exhibit a similar protective effect against ROS generated by high light stress [25]. However, taking advantage of photoluminescence (PL) properties to reduce high-intensity light exposure has been rarely reported. CDs have the potential to absorb excess light, which is detrimental to photosynthesis, surface defects CDs can introduce localized energy states, influencing absorption and emission characteristics [26,27]. Meanwhile, CDs can function as both electron donors and acceptors, facilitating energy transfer to chloroplasts and regulating electron transport within the photosynthetic system. As electron donors, CDs capture and transfer electrons due to their high surface area and electron-rich structure. CDs can also act as electron acceptors within the electron transport chain. Their presence enhances the conductivity of the chain, improving electron transport efficiency and promoting electron exchange between photosystems I and II. This process supports ATP production, oxygen generation, and the reduction cycles of water splitting and NADP to NADPH [28,29]. It has also been reported that doping CDs with elements such as magnesium or nitrogen can upregulate genes related to chlorophyll metabolism and promote chlorophyll synthesis [30]. Therefore, CDs present a promising solution for alleviating high-intensity light stress in microalgae such as C. pyrenoidosa, offering multiple benefits through their light-absorbing, electron-transfer, chlorophyll-enhancing, and ROS-scavenging properties. Additionally, transcriptomic studies have proven valuable in revealing the role of CDs in regulating gene expression in response to stress [31,32]. However, few reports exist on the application of CDs to resist high-intensity light stress and protect plant photosynthesis, let alone their potential gene regulatory effect.

In our study, we used citric acid and urea to synthesize water-soluble CDs, with their surfaces functionalized using NaOH [33]. The CDs, with a low fluorescence quantum efficiency (2.11 %) and a wide absorption range (300–700 nm), can absorb sunlight and alleviate the high-intensity light radiation experienced by C. pyrenoidosa. Furthermore, the surface functional groups of the CDs can bind to free radicals, enabling them to scavenge ROS. We systematically explored the regulatory mechanism of CDs on C. pyrenoidosa under high-intensity light stress through transcriptomics integrated with biochemical analysis. The results indicate that under high-intensity light stress, CDs activate plant hormones and MAPK signaling, initiate diterpenoid pathways and antioxidant processes to scavenge ROS, and trigger light absorption and heat dissipation pathways to enhance photosynthetic capacity. Ultimately, the biomass of C. pyrenoidosa was increased by 5.85 % (compared to that under normal light) and by 17.1 % (compared to that under high-intensity light), effectively alleviating high-intensity light stress (Fig. 1 ). This study demonstrates that the excellent light-absorbing, photoprotection, and ROS scavenger abilities of CDs can alleviate high-intensity light damage in plants, addressing biological challenges posed by extreme global environments.

The HRTEM images illustrate that the CDs exhibit typical microspherical characteristics, with an average diameter of approximately 3.88 nm ( Fig. 2
A-B). These CDs possess an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, matching the (100) basal spacing of graphite ( Fig. 2 C) [34]. The photoluminescence analysis (PL) reveals that the CDs emit maximum fluorescence at 469 nm when excited at 385 nm ( Fig. 2 D). Furthermore, the UV–vis spectrum demonstrates that the CDs have wide absorption (300–700 nm) in the visible range [35], with prominent absorption peaks at 335 nm and 555 nm, which are likely associated with the π → π* transition of the C = C conjugated structure and the n → π* transition between C = O and C = C [36–38], respectively ( Fig. 2 E). Meanwhile, the CDs have a low fluorescence quantum efficiency (2.11 %), indicating their potential to act as sunlight absorbers (
Fig. S1
) [39]. To evaluate the stability of the CDs in an ionic environment, they were dissolved in the ion-rich BG-11 medium. Notably, the position and intensity of the absorption peaks remained constant after 16 days of co-culture with the CDs and BG-11 medium, validating the resilience of the CDs against degradation or quenching in the BG-11 medium ( Fig. 2 F).

The chemical compositions and bonding details of the CDs were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As illustrated in Fig. S2 , the intense absorption peak at 3450 cm−1 arises from the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H. The absorption peak at 1705 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C = O and the bending vibration of N-H, while the peak at 1400 cm−1 is associated with the bending vibration of C-H and the stretching vibration of C-N. The presence of hydrophilic groups like O-H and N-H imparts exceptional hydrophilic properties to the CDs, facilitating their interaction with organisms. The full-band XPS spectrum (
Fig. S3
A) shows that the CDs possess C 1 s (285.46 eV, 59.84 %), N 1 s (399.99 eV, 13.14 %), O 1 s (531.57 eV, 24.35 %), and Na 1 s (1071.88 eV, 2.67 %). Furthermore, the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (
Fig. S3
B) reveals the existence of C = C/C-C, C = O, and C-N at 284.72 eV, 286.24 eV, and 287.93 eV, respectively [40]. The peaks at 398.42 eV and 287.93 eV in the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (
Fig. S3
C) are assigned to N-H and C-N, respectively [41], Additionally, the O 1 s spectrum (
Fig. S3
D) exhibits two peaks at 531.19 eV and 532.61 eV, attributed to C = O and C-O-H/C-O-C [40]. The result of XPS is consistent with the FT-IR result and further verifies the presence of hydrophilic groups in the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on the growth of C. pyrenoidosa under high-intensity light stress
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the effect of CDs on the growth of C. pyrenoidosa, we cultured C. pyrenoidosa in BG-11 medium to the logarithmic phase under different conditions, including (1) normal condition (CK: 4000 lux, wavelength between 400 and 700 nm), (2) high-intensity light stress (10000 lux, wavelength between 400 and 700 nm), and (3) high-intensity light stress with different concentrations of CDs (5, 10, 20, 40, 60, and 80 mg/L). Under high-intensity light stress, C. pyrenoidosa exhibited degreening, and its biomass decreased by 9.63 % compared to CK ( Fig. 3
A-B). The chlorophyll/carotenoid protein complex is the light-harvesting pigment-protein of C. pyrenoidosa [42]. Under high-intensity light stress, the contents of chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, total chlorophyll, and carotenoids were significantly reduced by 52.83 %, 53.41 %, 53.02 %, and 40.80 %, respectively, compared to CK ( Fig. 3 C-F). These results illustrate that high-intensity light stress destroys the light-harvesting pigment-protein of C. pyrenoidosa, causing severe damage to its photosystems. Additionally, carotenoids protect chlorophyll from peroxidation damage caused by high-intensity light stress. The decreased carotenoid content under high-intensity light stress suggests that the photo-protective systems are compromised, resulting in photoinhibition.

Based on the excellent hydrophilic properties and stability of CDs in the BG-11 medium, CDs at different concentrations were dissolved and co-cultured with C. pyrenoidosa to the logarithmic stage. The results demonstrate that the supplementation of CDs effectively mitigates the biomass reduction and chlorophyll depletion caused by photoinhibition, indicating that CDs can restore the photosynthetic capacity of C. pyrenoidosa damaged by high-intensity light stress. As shown in Fig. 3 B, the alleviation effect of CDs on C. pyrenoidosa growth is concentration-dependent. Biomass and chlorophyll content initially increase with rising CDs concentration, remaining higher than that under high-intensity light stress even at higher CDs concentrations (up to 80 mg/L). Furthermore, treatment with 40 mg/L CDs resulted in a 5.85 % increase in biomass compared to CK and a 17.1 % increase compared to the high-intensity light stress condition alone ( Fig. 3 A). Therefore, the CDs can alleviate the high-intensity light stress experienced by C. pyrenoidosa. Moreover, chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, total chlorophyll, and carotenoids also significantly increased by 2.24, 2.00, 1.42, and 2.17 times, respectively, with 40 mg/L CDs treatment, reaching peak values. Additionally, oxygen production, a product of photosynthesis, exhibited trends similar to those observed for pigment content and biomass ( Fig. 3 G). These findings are consistent with previous studies, showing that CDs enhance plant pigment content and photosynthetic activity, improving photosynthesis under stress conditions [30,35,43,44]. Based on these results, a CDs concentration of 40 mg/L was determined to be optimal for subsequent experiments.

To confirm the interaction between C. pyrenoidosa and CDs, confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was performed to analyze C. pyrenoidosa before and after CDs treatments. As shown in Fig. S4
A-B, under excitation at 405 nm, distinct red fluorescence originating from chloroplast autofluorescence was observed, along with blue fluorescence from CDs visible on the cell surfaces, indicating the binding of CDs to C. pyrenoidosa. Moreover, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) revealed no significant differences in the surface morphology or energy spectra of C, N, and O elements on cell surfaces between the group treated with CDs and the control group without CDs, suggesting that CDs do not damage the cell membrane of C. pyrenoidosa. Furthermore, the presence of the specific element Na from CDs was detected on the surface of C. pyrenoidosa in the energy spectrum (
Fig. S5
A-L), confirming the distribution of CDs on the cell surface. Additionally, as shown in Fig. S6 , CDs exhibit a negative charge, with a zeta potential of −6.49 mV, attributed to the presence of –COOH and –OH functional groups on their surface [45]. The zeta potentials of C. pyrenoidosa and CDs/C. pyrenoidosa was measured at −11.60 mV and −13.27 mV, respectively, indicating the binding of CDs to C. pyrenoidosa. According to Zhu et al. [46], negatively charged CDs can bind to the pectin in the cell wall. Therefore, CDs may bind to the cell wall of C. pyrenoidosa and adhere to its surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs absorb excessive sunlight and repair light damage caused by high-intensity light stress in C. pyrenoidosa
ce:section.ce:para:
The low quantum efficiency of CDs (2.11 %) and their wide absorption range (300–700 nm) in the visible spectrum give them the potential to absorb sunlight efficiently [35,39]. We evaluated the light absorption of C. pyrenoidosa before and after the addition of CDs. As depicted in Fig. 4
A-B, the light absorption by C. pyrenoidosa gradually decreased upon the addition of CDs, with optimal light absorption restored at 40 mg/L CDs. This reduction suggests that CDs attenuate light radiation intensity on C. pyrenoidosa by absorbing excess sunlight, thereby safeguarding the organism. However, substantial sunlight still reaches the surface of C. pyrenoidosa, which may disrupt energy distribution within photosystems.

To analyze the effect of CDs on the energy distribution of C. pyrenoidosa, we performed the Fast acquisition phase (Poly 300 ms) of the fast chlorophyll fluorescence kinetics curve using a Handy PEA chlorophyll fluorimeter (PAM-2500). As shown in Fig. 4 C, compared with CK, under high-intensity light stress, the light energy absorbed by the unit reaction center (ABS/RC) increased, indicating gradual saturation of the reaction center with energy. This results in reduced energy utilization for photosynthesis., This results in reduced energy utilization for photosynthesis, causing harmful “unused” excitation energy to accumulate in the photosynthetic membrane (see Table S1 for fluorescence parameter definitions). As a result, the excess light energy cannot be fully captured by the subordinate receptor QA, increasing the heat dissipation of DI0/RC ( Fig. 4 C). It has been noted that the photosystem can adjust its energy distribution by increasing heat dissipation to manage excessive energy and prevent damage from exciton accumulation [47,48]. Moreover, the energy transferred to QA (TR0/RC) increases while the energy transferred through QA (ET0/RC) does not show a significant increment ( Fig. 4 C). This leads to a reduced probability of excitons captured by QA being transferred to other electron acceptors beyond QA (ET0/TR0), indicating an excess accumulation of electrons at QA and subsequent reduction of QA ( Fig. 4 C). Studies have suggested that the likelihood of photodamage decreases when QA is oxidized and excitation energy is utilized for electron transport, whereas the risk of photodamage rises when QA is reduced [49]. Consequently, the decrease in ET0/TR0 suggests the occurrence of photodamage in C. pyrenoidosa under high-intensity light stress. Notably, the presence of CDs alters the energy distribution of photosynthesis. Specifically, CDs can decrease the value of ABS/RC and enhance the values of ET0/RC and ET0/TR0, thereby reducing the light absorption by reaction centers, regulating energy distribution, and safeguarding the reaction centers against damage ( Fig. 4 C).

High-intensity light stress can impair photosystem functionality, initiate a cascade of stress responses, and disrupt various cellular processes [50]. Fv/Fm, a crucial marker of chlorophyll fluorescence, is commonly used to assess photosynthetic efficiency. Under abiotic stresses, the Fv/Fm of photosynthetic organisms often decreases [51]. As depicted in Fig. 4 C-H, under high-intensity light stress, Fv/Fm, Y(II), ETR, and qP significantly decrease, while NPQ and Y(NO), which represent the quantum yield of non-regulated energy dissipation in PS II, increase due to excessive light. Light energy absorbed by the thylakoid light-harvesting proteins can either be used for photochemical purposes or dissipated as heat. Following treatment with CDs, Fv/Fm (Fig. 4C), Y(II), ETR, and qP ( Fig. 4 D-F) increase, while NPQ and Y(NO) ( Fig. 4 G-H) decrease, indicating more efficient use of light energy in the photochemical process. The reduction in Y(NO) suggests improved conversion efficiency of photochemical energy and enhanced protective capabilities of the photosystems due to CDs. Thus, under high-intensity light stress, CDs contribute to photoprotection and promote enhanced photosynthetic performance.

As a result, the CDs on the surface of C. pyrenoidosa act as light absorbers, mitigating excess light irradiation. They also prevent excessive reduction of QA due to electron accumulation, repair photosystems, and ultimately enhance photosynthesis ( Fig. 4 I).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant activity of CDs and alleviation of high-intensity light stress in C. pyrenoidosa
ce:section.ce:para:
In high-intensity light stresses, organisms accumulate excess reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as superoxide anion (O2 •−), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydroxyl radical (•OH), and singlet oxygen (1O2), leading to oxidative damage. CDs with their abundant unpaired electrons and surface defects, act as proton donors to scavenge free radicals [20]. This ability helps alleviate biological stress and protect organisms from exogenous stress by scavenging excessive ROS. In this study, we utilized potassium permanganate (KMnO4) to assess the antioxidant capacity of CDs. As shown in Fig. S7
A, the reduction rate of KMnO4 increases with the concentration of CDs, exhibiting their antioxidant capacity. Further tests revealed that CDs exhibit concentration-dependent free radical scavenging. At 80 mg/L, CDs showed reduction rates of 35.0 %, 80.0 %, and 63.9 % for 1,1-Diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH•), O2 •− and •OH radicals, respectively (
Fig. S7
B-D). Previous studies suggest that free radical scavenging by CDs primarily involves hydrogen atom transfer from hydrophilic surface groups to free radicals, forming less reactive or inactive substances [52,53]. Specifically, -NH2, -OH, and other surface functional groups on CDs provide hydrogen atoms that combine with DPPH•, O2 •− and •OH radicals, resulting in DPPH, H2O2, and H2O. Given the prevalence of O2 •− and •OH as harmful free radicals in biological systems, along with the effective scavenging activity of CDs against these radicals, we propose that CDs hold significant promise for mitigating abiotic stresses [54,55].

Under exposure to high-intensity light stress, intracellular ROS and O2 •− levels escalate, and •OH degradation rate decreases (Fig. 5
A-C). Additionally, malondialdehyde (MDA), a biomarker of lipid peroxidation, significantly increases, indicating oxidative damage to C. pyrenoidosa induced by high-intensity light stress ( Fig. 5 D). To mitigate oxidative damage, organisms possess a complex antioxidant defense system comprising enzymatic (e.g., SOD, CAT, and POD) and non-enzymatic (phenolic, flavonoid, ascorbic acid, and glutathione) components. These systems collectively regulate cellular redox balance and manage ROS levels to ensure cellular health [56]. Notably, superoxide dismutase (SOD) enzymes located in the thylakoid membrane play a crucial role in dismutating O2 •− into H2O2. Despite a 22.4 % increase in SOD activity, the O2 •− content in C. pyrenoidosa increased by 5.3 % under high-intensity light stress conditions ( Fig. 5 B, E), indicating the organism's antioxidant defense system may be insufficient to cope with high-intensity light-induced stress. However, CDs hold promise in enhancing C. pyrenoidosa's antioxidant defense system against ROS generated during high-intensity light stress. This enhancement could effectively protect cells from oxidative damage and maintain cellular health under adverse environmental conditions [24,31].

The addition of CDs to C. pyrenoidosa enhances its antioxidant defense system in a concentration-dependent manner, similar to the effects observed on biomass and photosynthetic pigments. Enzyme activity increased initially and then decreased, with rising concentrations of CDs, with the best effect achieved at 40 mg/L, though a beneficial effect remains at 80 mg/L ( Fig. 5 ). This is because high concentrations of CDs can interfere with plant cell homeostasis [44,57,58]. As shown in Fig. 5 E, compared to high-intensity light stress alone, the 40 mg/L CDs treatment increases the activity of SOD in C. pyrenoidosa by 24.4 %, and a substantial portion of O2 •− was effectively degraded into H2O2, leading to a significant 12.1 % reduction in O2 •− levels ( Fig. 5 B). Subsequently, H2O2 can be reduced to H2O by catalase (CAT), ascorbate peroxidase (APX), and peroxidase (POD) enzymes. As shown in Fig. 5 F-H, applying 40 mg/L CDs during high-intensity light stress minimally impacted the activity of the POD enzyme but notably boosted the activities of CAT and APX enzymes by 136.0 % and 81.5 %, respectively.

The ascorbic acid-glutathione (AsA-GSH) cycle plays a critical role in maintaining the balance of ROS and cellular redox homeostasis [59]. Both AsA and GSH are crucial in scavenging ROS, mediating cellular components, and mitigating oxidative damage under abiotic stresses [13,60]. Within the AsA-GSH cycle, AsA serves as the substrate for the reaction in which APX reduces H2O2 to H2O, while AsA can be oxidized into dehydroascorbic acid (DHA) by APX [61]. As shown in Fig. 5 I-K, under high-intensity light stress, the generation of antioxidants such as AsA, and AsA + DHA is stimulated, accompanied by an increase in the AsA/DHA ratio, reflecting the self-protective response of C. pyrenoidosa to the high-intensity light stress. However, the antioxidant defense system of C. pyrenoidosa proves insufficient in resisting the oxidative damage induced by intense light stress. Upon adding CDs, the values of AsA + DHA, AsA, and the AsA/DHA ratio surge by 62.2 %, 73.4 %, and 19.7 %, respectively. These findings indicate the enhancement of DHA conversion into AsA and the improvement of antioxidant capacity, which is also associated with the augmented APX activity. Furthermore, DHA can be converted to AsA with the involvement of dehydroascorbate reductase (DHAR) and GSH, ultimately eliminating excess H2O2. Fig. 5 L illustrates that CDs also bolster the capacity of the antioxidant system by promoting the expression of antioxidant GSH by 32.9 %. GSH also plays a vital role in scavenging hydroxyl radicals (•OH) by pairing with them using its sulfhydryl group (−SH), reducing their potent oxidative potential. The elevation in GSH levels induced by CDs signifies an enhanced ability to scavenge •OH), leading to a degradation rate of •OH increasing by 13.7 % ( Fig. 5 C), ultimately reducing the content of •OH in C. pyrenoidosa.

As a result, CDs substantially contribute to the antioxidant defense system of C. pyrenoidosa by scavenging ROS. CDs not only enhance the activities of SOD, CAT, and APX enzymes but also elevate the levels of AsA and GSH in the AsA-GSH cycle, thereby reducing the generation of ROS (by 52.01 %) and MDA (88.7 %), alleviating oxidative stress triggered by high-intensity light stress. Consistent with our findings, previous studies have shown that CDs can effectively protect plants from oxidative stress by inhibiting the accumulation of ROS (H2O2, •OH, and O2 •–), thereby maintaining the normal physiological functions of cells [43,62].

The DEGs identified in the transcriptome analysis were further analyzed using Gene Ontology (GO) and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) functional pathway analysis to elucidate the molecular mechanisms underlying the response of C. pyrenoidosa to CDs under high-intensity light stress. The 751 DEGs were classified into three primary categories: biological processes (BP), cellular components (CC), and molecular functions (MF). These categories were further divided into 42 subgroups to clarify their functions. As shown in Fig. S10 , the DEGs were involved in processes such as “metabolic process,” “regulation of biological processes,” “signal,” and “response to stimulus.” Additionally, many DEGs exhibited antioxidant activity. Therefore, CDs have the potential to alleviate high-intensity light-induced stress in C. pyrenoidosa by activating signaling pathways and modulating metabolic and biological processes [31,32,54].

Considering that high-intensity light stress can damage chloroplast function in C. pyrenoidosa, we conducted a GO term enrichment analysis focusing on chloroplast-related terms (
Table S3
). As shown in Fig. S11 , a total of 110 DEGs (including duplicate genes) were enriched in 16 GO terms associated with chloroplasts, spanning 4 biological processes, 10 cellular components, and 2 molecular functions (
Fig. S11
A). Notably, Fig. S11
B shows that genes involved in chlorophyll biosynthesis and catabolism are significantly up-regulated by CDs, indicating that CDs enhance chlorophyll metabolism in C. pyrenoidosa under high-intensity light stress. Additionally, these DEGs primarily localize to chloroplasts, chloroplast thylakoid membranes, and chloroplast thylakoids (
Fig. S11
A). Additionally, 5 genes of chlorophyll binding are significantly up-regulated under high-intensity light stress with CDs treatment, suggesting enhanced chlorophyll binding function, which may facilitate improved photosynthetic capacity in C. pyrenoidosa (
Fig. S11
C) [63,64].

The KEGG functional pathway analysis of C. pyrenoidosa transcript profiles exposed to CDs revealed the enrichment of several pathways, as depicted in Fig. S12 and Table S4 . The affected pathways include the MAPK signaling pathway-plant, neuroactive ligand-receptor interaction, porphyrin metabolism, plant hormone signal transduction, and diterpenoid biosynthesis. Plants respond to high-intensity light stress by initiating signal transduction from chloroplasts to the entire cell, triggering various physiological and biochemical reactions to mitigate its harmful effects. The MAPK signaling pathway-plant, plant hormone signal transduction, and diterpenoid biosynthesis pathways are known to play crucial roles in plant stress responses, suggesting that these pathways may be key mechanisms through which CDs alleviate high-intensity light stress in C. pyrenoidosa [65–67]. Additionally, we focused on eight pathways related to plant stress resistance and photosynthesis. Fig. 6
A illustrates the significant enrichment of these pathways, which include stress responses (MAPK signaling pathway-plant, plant hormone signal transduction, and diterpenoid biosynthesis) as well as photosynthesis-related processes (carbon fixation in photosynthetic organisms, photosynthesis-antenna proteins, carotenoid biosynthesis, fructose, and mannose metabolism, and the pentose phosphate pathway).

Diterpenoid metabolites, such as gibberellin (GA), play a pivotal role in mediating growth and stress resilience in plants [15,68,69]. GA is known to be light-sensitive, decreasing under high-intensity light conditions and increasing under low-light conditions [70,71]. In our study, the up-regulation of the GA20ox gene in the CDs group compared to the CK group suggests a reduction in cellular irradiation and enhancement of stress resilience in C. pyrenoidosa ( Fig. 6 A). The chlorophyll-binding antenna proteins pathway includes internal antenna light-harvesting proteins (light-harvesting complex II isoform, LHCb2), which enhance the efficiency of light energy absorption [72]. Carotenoids, structural components of the photosynthetic apparatus, form pigment-protein complexes with LHCs [73]. The decreased expression of LHCB2 and lcyB in C. pyrenoidosa with CDs treatment under high-intensity light stress ( Fig. 6 B) further supports CDs' role in mitigating surface radiation [74]. Photosynthesis comprises light reactions responsible for light energy absorption, conversion, and transmission, and carbon reactions facilitating carbon assimilation and carbohydrate ((CH2O)n) accumulation. Therefore, CDs enhance the light reaction process, directing energy toward the Calvin cycle. The significant up-regulation of fructose-bisphosphate aldolase (ALDO) and glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH) gene expression strengthens the Calvin cycle [75,76]. Moreover, CDs influence pathways related to carbohydrates such as fructose and mannose metabolism, and the pentose phosphate pathway ( Fig. 6 A).

In response to fluctuations in light intensity, photosynthetic organisms treated with CDs undergo state transition processes that enhance photosynthetic yield and mitigate photodamage. As depicted in Fig. 6 A-B, the enrichment of serine/threonine-protein kinase (CTR1) in the MAPK signaling and plant hormone signal transduction pathways, up-regulated by CDs, enhances resistance to high-intensity light. This activation accelerates the phosphorylation of the light-harvesting complex of PSII (LHCII). As a result, LHCII migrates from PSII to PSI, restoring the balance of light excitation energy between the photosystems and maintaining the redox poise of the photosynthetic electron transport chain [77].

The results highlight that CDs activate signal transduction pathways (MAPK and plant hormones) under high-intensity light stress, triggering photosynthesis and antioxidant processes. Specifically, CDs downregulate the gene expression of CpLHCb2 and CplcyB while upregulating CpGA20ox in C. pyrenoidosa. This regulatory response reduces the impact of radiation on the organism's surface, causing the displacement of LHCII to the unstacked region of the thylakoid membrane. Consequently, this relocation enhances the conversion of energy from PSII to PSI, improving energy conversion efficiency [78]. Furthermore, the upregulation of CpCTR1 accelerates phosphorylation, which reduces the accumulation of excess excitons at QA, as evidenced by increased ET0/TR0 ratios. This adjustment helps mitigate strong light stress by enhancing carbon fixation, thereby increasing carbohydrate production fueled by surplus energy from photosynthesis. Notably, the upregulated gene expression of CpALDO and CpGAPDH contributes significantly to this process. Additionally, both CpCTR1 and CpGA20ox play crucial roles in mitigating intense light stress through their upregulation, as illustrated in Fig. 6 B.

In this study, we synthesized water-soluble CDs and applied them to C. pyrenoidosa under high-intensity light stress. These CDs exhibit low quantum efficiency and a broad absorption spectrum, allowing them to efficiently absorb sunlight on the surface of C. pyrenoidosa. Additionally, the surface functional groups of the CDs can bind to free radicals, enabling them to scavenge ROS. Under high-intensity light stress, CDs activate plant hormones and MAPK signals of C. pyrenoidosa, leading to reduced light absorption and increased heat dissipation, thereby enhancing photosynthetic capacity. Furthermore, this signaling also induced diterpenoid pathways and antioxidant processes, further facilitating ROS scavenging. Ultimately, these mechanisms contributed to an increase in the biomass of C. pyrenoidosa, boosting its resilience to high-intensity light stress.
